sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastroenteritis causes acute diarrhoea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, and sometimes fever. Prehospital care should prioritise assessment of dehydration, exclusion of alternate serious causes, infection-control measures, and appropriate escalation for severe illness.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Acute diarrhoea, vomiting, nausea, or abdominal cramping
- Fever, malaise, or myalgia
- Signs of dehydration such as thirst, dry mucosa, tachycardia, reduced urine output, dizziness, or orthostatic symptoms
- Blood or mucus in stool, severe pain, or persistent vomiting in higher-risk cases

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Gastroenteritis is highly transmissible, so infection-control precautions matter
- Consider surgical abdomen, sepsis, pregnancy-related pathology, toxic ingestion, or cardiac causes if the presentation is atypical
- Antimotility agents should be avoided in bloody diarrhoea, high fever, or suspected invasive bacterial illness
- Outbreaks in remote, residential, or workplace settings may require local public health or workplace notification
- Frailty, pregnancy, significant comorbidity, or inability to maintain hydration should lower the threshold for transport

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and full vital sign survey, including blood glucose where relevant
- Assess hydration, perfusion, abdominal pain severity, and ability to tolerate oral intake
- Encourage oral rehydration only if tolerated and not provoking ongoing vomiting
- Consider simple analgesia or antipyretic treatment for discomfort or fever
- Advise rest, hygiene precautions, and avoidance of food handling or return to communal duties until symptoms have settled according to local policy

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access and IV fluids when dehydration is clinically significant, perfusion is impaired, or oral intake is failing
- Consider antiemetic treatment within scope for persistent vomiting
- Loperamide may be considered only in carefully selected adults without blood in stool, high fever, or suspected invasive infection, and only if consistent with local protocol
- Antibiotics should not be routine and should only follow medical direction when a specific bacterial syndrome is suspected
- Escalate early for severe pain, bloody stool, reduced conscious state, sepsis concern, or persistent inability to maintain hydration

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Bloody stool, severe dehydration, reduced consciousness, pregnancy, frailty, or significant comorbidity should lower the threshold for transport
- Norovirus and foodborne illness are common causes; antibiotics do not help uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis
